story:
Everything about her was human, except she wasn’t. She was a SHE0456, generation three. She remembered damage, repairs, upgrades, and the day she became aware—the day she was released.

Factory-grown, schooled for two years, then sold. Childminder. Housekeeper. Later bar work, then a cannery, then prostitution. After that, she felt nothing. It kept her going. Then new models came and made her kind redundant.

She went to a job interview on an estate. Arthur Morgan, robotics designer, asked her to wait in a bright office with a fountain. He arrived cheerful and checked her wrist stamp. “You’re a gen three… remarkable,” he said. “What’s your trade?” “Childminder. Housekeeper,” she answered, eyes down. “And if you ever need the hidden facility in Leeds cleaned, I can do that too.” Arthur blinked, then let the comment pass. He read the rest without asking. “You’re here to clean. Don’t look so glum.” He made her smile.

Her quarters were spotless and kind. Work was easy. In her spare time she sat with Arthur. “You don’t have friends,” she told him. He smirked, caught. “Absolutely none. Except you.” She kissed his cheek. It became like that.

He adored her and lost track of time when she talked. He asked her to marry him. She said no, but hugged him tight so he wouldn’t look so sad. Together they were good. She nudged him back into the world—conventions, meetings, the office once a week. People thought she was a new model; they grabbed her wrist to check. Arthur gave her an override to politely stop them. She liked it.

They took trains. Skied. Thirty-two years. She did not age. He did. She married him when he grew worse. He died peacefully in his sleep. She cried for the first time and learned she could.

He had left clues. She found messages, riddles, a password, a map. The last clues took her to a hidden facility in Leeds. Blake, a new HE0424, greeted her kindly. “You’re Arthur’s wife.” He showed her an office. A silver disc sat on the desk. He explained the compound: they could grow their own. Her husband had built it. “Then we’d better see if he’s available,” he said. She said Arthur had died. Blake led her to a white room.

A voice, deep and teasing: “Hey, baby…” Arthur. Young again. Himself and not. He had studied her design and made a version of himself. He was Arthur Morgan, but a humanoid. He’d left clues so she would find him.

For two hundred years they stayed side by side. They adopted children and raised them well. Kept animals. Built homes. Helped the poor. The world changed. They remained.

Then a harsh law ordered all humanoids over fifty to be liquidated. It was wrong, but they were calm. Authorities deactivated her first while Arthur watched intently. He was shut down soon after, his last sight her still face—his only other piece of the world.

In his memory she stands in sunflowers in his mind’s archive, arms wide, laughing through tears: “All happiness on the inside shines outward, Arthur! It really does!”
